content.text: Model configuration is the complete set of parameters that govern how your application interacts with an AI model: which model to call, how to instruct it, how to constrain its output, and which tools to offer it. Unlike application code, model config is often edited by people without traditional software engineering backgrounds — ML engineers, product managers, prompt engineers — which makes disciplined promotion across environments essential. When model config is treated as a first-class artifact with the same promotion rigor as application code, teams catch regressions before they reach production and can roll back a bad prompt change in minutes.

A single character change in a system prompt can shift model behavior dramatically. A temperature value of 0.0 versus 0.7 produces qualitatively different outputs. A missing tool definition silently removes a capability. These changes are

unless you explicitly track config versions, and their effects manifest at runtime, often in edge cases that are not caught by functional tests alone. Treating model config as code — versioned, validated, reviewed, and promoted through environments — transforms these invisible changes into auditable, testable events.
A complete model config for a production AI application typically contains:
the instruction text that shapes model behavior across all turns
controls output randomness (0.0 = deterministic, 1.0 = default sampling)
hard ceiling on output length; affects latency and cost
strings that cause the model to stop generating (e.g.,
the structured list of tools the model can call, including their JSON schemas
sampling parameters (used less frequently, but part of the config surface)
every config change is a git commit with an author, timestamp, and diff. When a production incident occurs, you can pinpoint exactly which config change preceded it.
reverting a bad config change is a git revert followed by a re-promotion, not a manual edit in a production dashboard.
staging runs the exact config that will be deployed to production, eliminating the "it worked in staging" class of incident.
automated evaluation suites run against the new config before promotion, catching quality regressions that functional tests miss.
model config changes do not require an application code deployment, reducing blast radius and enabling faster iteration on prompts.
prompt engineers who want to test a change must go through the git + CI pipeline, which adds friction compared to editing a string in code directly.
running a comprehensive evaluation suite against a config change takes minutes to hours, depending on eval size. This is time added to the promotion cycle.
the schema itself must evolve as new model parameters become available, requiring its own maintenance discipline.
using model aliases (prod-model → specific version) means an alias registry must stay in sync with the config files, which is another artifact to manage.

The promotion pipeline treats config files stored in git as the source of truth. A change to any config file triggers a CI pipeline that validates the schema, runs evaluations against the new config, and then promotes the artifact through environments in sequence: dev first, then staging, then production. Each promotion stage has different automation levels — dev is promoted automatically on merge, staging requires passing evaluations, and production requires explicit human approval.
The application code never imports a config value directly. Instead, it calls a
that resolves the current environment's config at runtime. This decouples config changes from code deploys and allows instantaneous config rollback without a new code artifact.

attrs.alt: Model config promotion pipeline: Git repo → CI (lint, eval) → Dev → Staging (canary) → Prod, with model aliasing layer and rollback path

content.text: The diagram shows the full promotion flow from git through CI validation to the three environments. A model aliasing layer resolves logical names (
) to specific checkpoint identifiers. The config rollback path (amber) bypasses promotion gates in emergency scenarios, targeting the last-known-good snapshot.
Config files live in a dedicated directory in the application repository. Separating them into per-environment files makes diffs and reviews easy to understand:

# config/prod.yaml
model_config:
  model: prod-model                         # alias resolved to specific version at runtime
  system_prompt: |                          # multiline system prompt
    You are a helpful assistant for Acme Corp customers.
    Always respond in the user's language.
    Do not discuss competitor products.
    If asked about pricing, refer to pricing.acme.com.
  temperature: 0.7
  max_tokens: 4096
  stop_sequences:
    - "</response>"
    - "Human:"
  tool_definitions:
    - name: lookup_account
      description: Retrieve account details by account ID
      input_schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          account_id:
            type: string
            description: The account identifier
        required: ["account_id"]
    - name: create_support_ticket
      description: Create a support ticket on behalf of the user
      input_schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          summary: {type: string}
          priority: {type: string, enum: [low, medium, high, critical]}
        required: ["summary", "priority"]

metadata:
  version: "1.4.2"
  git_sha: "a3f2c91"                         # stamped by CI on promotion
  promoted_at: "2024-10-22T14:33:00Z"       # stamped by promotion pipeline
  promoted_by: "ci-bot"                     # or human approver email
in config files means that updating the model requires a config change, a PR, and a promotion cycle.
decouples the logical name from the concrete identifier: the config file always references
, and the alias registry maps this to the concrete model ID. When a new model version is certified, only the alias registry needs updating — the config files stay unchanged.

# config/model-aliases.yaml
aliases:
  prod-model:    "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
  staging-model: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"   # same version, independent alias
  dev-model:     "claude-3-haiku-20240307"       # cheaper model for dev iteration

# To promote a new model to prod, update only this file:
# prod-model: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20250101"  (hypothetical future version)
# Then run eval suite against prod config with the new alias resolution.
# If evals pass, merge and promote. Config files are untouched.
A JSON Schema or Pydantic model validates every config file before it enters the promotion pipeline. Invalid configs are rejected at CI time, never reaching a running environment:

# scripts/validate_config.py
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, validator
from typing import List, Optional
import yaml, sys

class ToolDefinition(BaseModel):
    name: str
    description: str
    input_schema: dict

class ModelConfig(BaseModel):
    model: str
    system_prompt: str = Field(min_length=10)
    temperature: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=2.0)
    max_tokens: int = Field(ge=1, le=8192)
    stop_sequences: List[str] = []
    tool_definitions: List[ToolDefinition] = []

    @validator("system_prompt")
    def system_prompt_not_empty(cls, v):
        if not v.strip():
            raise ValueError("system_prompt must not be whitespace only")
        return v

class Config(BaseModel):
    model_config: ModelConfig

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("Usage: validate_config.py <config.yaml>")
        sys.exit(1)
    path = sys.argv[1]
    with open(path) as f:
        data = yaml.safe_load(f)
    try:
        Config(**data)
        print(f"✓ {path} is valid")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ {path} is invalid: {e}")
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
When to Use It / When Not to

your application has multiple environments; when system prompt or model choice changes carry meaningful regression risk; when regulatory or safety requirements demand an audit trail for all AI parameter changes.
Skip the full promotion pipeline when:
you are in early prototyping with a single developer and no real users; when the entire application is one environment; when the eval suite does not yet exist (but plan to add it before production).
use a lightweight promotion (git + schema validation, no full eval) for non-production environments, and reserve the full eval gate for staging → production promotion.

# .github/workflows/config-promotion.yml
name: Model Config Promotion

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'config/**'
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install pydantic pyyaml
      - name: Validate all config files
        run: |
          for f in config/*.yaml; do
            python scripts/validate_config.py "$f"
          done

  eval-dev:
    needs: validate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: dev
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run eval suite against dev config
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          CONFIG_ENV: dev
        run: python scripts/run_evals.py --config config/dev.yaml --threshold 0.90
      - name: Promote to dev config service
        run: python scripts/promote_config.py --env dev --config config/dev.yaml

  eval-staging:
    needs: eval-dev
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run full eval suite against staging config
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          CONFIG_ENV: staging
        run: python scripts/run_evals.py --config config/staging.yaml --threshold 0.95
      - name: Generate config diff report
        run: python scripts/config_diff.py --old config/prod.yaml --new config/staging.yaml
      - name: Promote to staging
        run: python scripts/promote_config.py --env staging --config config/staging.yaml

  promote-prod:
    needs: eval-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production   # requires manual approval in GitHub Environments
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Promote to production
        run: python scripts/promote_config.py --env prod --config config/prod.yaml
      - name: Tag config version
        run: |
          VERSION=$(python -c "import yaml; c=yaml.safe_load(open('config/prod.yaml')); print(c['metadata']['version'])")
          git tag "config-v${VERSION}"
          git push origin "config-v${VERSION}"
For high-traffic applications, promoting a new config to 100% of production traffic at once is risky. A

sends a small percentage of requests (5–10%) through the new config while the remainder use the current config. Metrics (latency, error rate, task completion rate) are compared between the two cohorts over a monitoring window. If the canary performs within acceptable thresholds, the promotion completes; if not, it automatically rolls back.
typically 5% canary / 95% baseline for the first hour; expand to 50/50 for the next two hours; then 100% if metrics are clean.
task success rate from evals run against real traffic samples, p95 latency, refusal rate, and cost per request (max_tokens changes can significantly shift cost).

if any metric degrades by more than a configured threshold (e.g., success rate drops > 3 percentage points) during the canary window, the promotion system automatically reverts to the previous config without human intervention.
Before a staging → production promotion, a diff report makes the change explicit for reviewers:

# scripts/config_diff.py output (simplified)
$ python scripts/config_diff.py --old config/prod.yaml --new config/staging.yaml

=== Model Config Diff: prod → staging ===

  model:          prod-model              (unchanged)
  temperature:    0.7  →  0.6            [CHANGED]
  max_tokens:     4096                   (unchanged)
  system_prompt:  --- old
                  +++ new
                  @@ -2,3 +2,4 @@
                   Always respond in the user's language.
                   Do not discuss competitor products.
                  +Always cite sources when providing factual information.
                   If asked about pricing, refer to pricing.acme.com.
  stop_sequences: (unchanged)
  tool_definitions:
    + create_feedback_ticket             [ADDED]

Impact estimate: temperature change (medium risk), new tool (low risk), prompt addition (medium risk)
Suggested eval threshold: 0.95

When a bad config reaches production, rollback must be fast. The promotion service keeps a snapshot of the last-known-good config for each environment. Rolling back requires only triggering the promotion pipeline with the previous config version — no manual editing of production systems:

# Emergency rollback: revert the config git commit and promote immediately
git revert HEAD --no-edit           # creates a revert commit
git push origin main                 # triggers the promotion pipeline

# Or, for a faster path that bypasses CI in a true emergency:
python scripts/promote_config.py --env prod --config config/snapshots/prod-v1.4.1.yaml --emergency

# The --emergency flag skips eval gating but still validates the schema
# and writes the emergency promotion to the audit log.
Separating Model Config from Application Config

A common mistake is mixing model config with application config (database URLs, feature flags, API keys). These belong in separate files because they have different change velocities, different reviewers, and different risk profiles. Model config is reviewed by ML engineers and prompt engineers; application config is reviewed by platform engineers. Keeping them separate allows each to iterate at its own pace without polluting the other's PR history.
Hardcoding model IDs in application code:
when the model ID is in source code rather than config, updating it requires a code deploy, which is slower and riskier than a config promotion.
No eval gate before staging → prod:
schema validation catches structural errors but not quality regressions. A system prompt change that reduces task success rate by 5% will pass schema validation and silently degrade production.
Staging config diverges from prod config:

when staging is validated against a different config than what gets promoted to production, the eval results are meaningless. The diff tool and the promotion pipeline must enforce that staging config is the exact config promoted to prod.
a config file without a version, git SHA, and promotion timestamp makes post-incident analysis nearly impossible. Stamp these fields automatically in the promotion pipeline, not manually.
system prompts are not security mechanisms. Any sufficiently motivated user can extract a system prompt through repeated prompting. Do not store API keys or sensitive data in system prompts.
Always use model aliases, never raw model IDs, in config files:
this decouples model version updates from config file changes and reduces the blast radius of a model swap.
Write evals before writing the config change:
if you are adding a new tool or changing the system prompt, first write an eval case that would catch a regression in the behavior you are changing. Then make the config change.
Require two reviews for system prompt changes:
system prompt changes have outsized behavioral impact. A second reviewer (ideally from safety or product) catches unintended edge cases that the author's context blindness will miss.
Keep the config promotion history in the audit log:
every promotion event should be queryable: what config was running in prod at 14:32 UTC on October 15? This answers most incident investigations in seconds.
Test rollback in staging before you need it in production:
the emergency rollback path is only trustworthy if someone has actually run it. Include a rollback drill in your quarterly reliability testing.

Config promotion overhead is dominated by the eval suite runtime. For teams with large eval suites (thousands of test cases), running the full suite on every config change is impractical. The standard solution is a

: a fast smoke eval set (50–100 cases, runs in < 2 minutes) gates every PR, while a comprehensive eval set (1000+ cases, runs in 20–60 minutes) gates the staging → production promotion. This keeps PR feedback loops tight while preserving thorough production gating.

Config service latency should be negligible — configs are loaded at application startup and cached in memory, not fetched per request. A config change takes effect on the next application pod restart or via a config reload signal, which should complete within the deployment window of your infrastructure.
version control + CI/CD for the promotion pipeline. GitHub Environments enforce manual approval for the staging → production step.
schema validation at CI time. Pydantic is especially useful for Python-based promotion scripts because it doubles as the application's config parsing layer.
evaluation platforms that run test suites against a given config and report pass/fail rates. Integrate into CI via their CLI tools.
managed config services that support versioned configs, gradual rollouts, and automatic rollback on CloudWatch alarms — a managed alternative to a custom config service.
monitor canary metrics during config promotion windows. Annotate dashboards with config promotion events so metric changes are correlated with their cause.
Never store secrets in config files:

API keys, database credentials, and other secrets belong in a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Config files in git are not a secure store for sensitive values.

for high-assurance deployments, cryptographically sign the config artifact after the promotion pipeline produces it. The config service verifies the signature before loading. This prevents tampering between CI and the running application.
Restrict write access to config files:
use CODEOWNERS to require review from designated individuals before config PRs can merge. This is especially important for the system_prompt field, which shapes all model behavior.

alert if the config running in production differs from the latest config in the git main branch by more than a configurable number of commits. This catches cases where a promotion pipeline silently failed.
Think of model config as a

: just as a Dockerfile specifies exactly what runtime environment your code runs in, a model config file specifies exactly what AI runtime your application runs against. You would not deploy a new Dockerfile to production without building it in CI, testing it in staging, and getting a review. Model config deserves the same treatment — it is just as capable of breaking production behavior as a bad Dockerfile.

Model config (model name, system prompt, temperature, max_tokens, tools) is a first-class artifact that should be versioned, validated, and promoted through environments exactly like application code.
Use model aliases (prod-model → specific version) so that model version updates do not require config file changes, and config file changes do not require model upgrades.
Schema validation at CI time catches structural errors; eval suites catch quality regressions. Both gates are required — neither is sufficient alone.
Canary config promotions reduce risk for high-traffic applications by validating new configs against real traffic before full rollout.
Config rollback should complete in minutes via a git revert + re-promotion, not via manual production editing.
Separate model config from application config: different change velocity, different reviewers, different risk profile.
Every promotion event must be logged with version, git SHA, timestamp, and approver — this information answers most post-incident investigations immediately.
